desc: Valley carpenter bee — LA's largest native bee (25mm). Extreme sexual dimorphism: males golden-furred ('teddy bear bee'), females jet black with metallic violet wing sheen. Excavates galleries in dead wood, fence posts, and structural timber. Buzz pollinates tomatoes and peppers. Males territorial but stingless.

fm.Size: ⅔ in–1 in; among largest NA bees
fm.Nesting: Tunnels in dead wood; sawdust partitions

hp: Buzz pollinator — vibrates flowers for pollen. On tomato. Metallic coloring.

desc: California carpenter bee — both sexes dark metallic blue-black (unlike the dimorphic Valley carpenter bee). Nests in dead yucca and agave stalks. Strong buzzy flight.
hp: Agave; yucca. Associated with Desert Agave and Our Lord's Candle.

desc: Globe mallow bee — medium bee that specializes on Malvaceae (mallow family) flowers. Nests in dense aggregations in sandy soil, sometimes thousands of burrows together.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers and garden plants. On Malvaceae (mallows).

desc: Sunflower chimney bee — builds distinctive mud chimney turrets over nest entrances (diagnostic field mark). Specialist on sunflower and related composites.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Helianthus (sunflower) and Asteraceae. Ground-nesting. On Asteraceae (composites). Sunflower Chimney.

desc: Pallid digger bee — large fast-flying desert digger bee. Important pollinator of native desert wildflowers. Males patrol territories aggressively. Sand-nesting.

fm.Nesting: Deep ground burrows in desert sand

hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Preys on ant. Important pollinator of native desert wildflowers.

desc: Ultra green sweat bee — brilliant metallic emerald green bee. Ground-nesting in communal aggregations, sometimes in lawn edges and pathways. One of LA's most photogenic bees.

fm.Hair: Metallic green head/thorax; striped abdomen (♀) or green (♂)

hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Preys on ant. Metallic coloring.

desc: Furrow bee — the most common native bee in LA County. Weakly eusocial with queen and worker castes. Ground-nesting. Visits an enormous range of flowers. Named for head furrow.

fm.Hair: Dark; faint metallic sheen; thin pale bands

hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. Most Common.

desc: Tripartite sweat bee — small metallic sweat bee, among the most abundant native bees in California. Weakly eusocial — queens found nests and produce worker daughters.
hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Preys on ant. Nest associate. On California.

desc: California leafcutter — cuts precise circular and oblong leaf pieces to line nest cells in cavities. Ventral abdominal pollen brush (scopa) diagnostic of family — carries pollen on belly, not legs. Important pollinator of alfalfa and wildflowers. Builds linear cell series in tubes.

fm.Hair: Dark; pale bands; orange ventral scopa when loaded
fm.Nesting: Cavity nester; bee hotels; leaf-lined cells

hp: On alfalfa (Medicago sativa) — agricultural associate. Nest associate.

desc: Blue orchard mason bee — excellent early-season pollinator far more efficient per-bee than honey bees. Nests in cavities partitioned with mud. Easy to attract with mason bee houses.

fm.Nesting: Cavity nester; mud cell partitions; bee hotels

hp: Blue orchard bee — most effective native orchard pollinator. 10x more efficient per bee than honeybees on apple/cherry (Bosch & Kemp 2001).

desc: European wool carder bee — introduced and now widespread. Males aggressively territorial — the only bee that physically rams and kills other bees to defend flower patches. Females card (scrape) plant trichomes for nest material. Larger than native Anthidium. Cavity nester.

fm.Size: ♂: ½ in–⅔ in. ♀: ⅓ in–½ in.
fm.Hair: Black with bold yellow lateral spots
fm.Nesting: Cavity nester; cells lined with carded plant hair

hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Cavity/stem nester. Preys on ant.

desc: Plum mining bee — medium mining bee that nests in soil with distinctive turret entrances. Specialist on Prunus (cherry, plum) blossoms in early spring.
hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. On Prunus (cherry).

desc: Complex mining bee — large conspicuous spring bee visiting willows and early Asteraceae. One of the first bees active each year. Ground-nesting in aggregations.

fm.Hair: Dark; dense tawny thorax; pale scopae

hp: On willows (salix) in riparian habitat. Associated with Red Willow.

desc: Pugnacious leafcutter — large robust leafcutter bee with conspicuous buzzy flight. Cuts large circular leaf pieces for nest lining. Named for aggressive nest defense.

fm.Hair: Dark; dense grayish-white thorax; pale bands

hp: Native wildflower and crop pollinator. Leafcutter — lines cells with leaf pieces. Nest associate.

desc: Squash bee — specialist pollinator of Cucurbita (squash, pumpkin, gourd). Active at dawn before honeybees, completing pollination by mid-morning. Males sleep inside closed squash flowers. Ground-nesting directly below cucurbit vines. Co-evolved with cultivated squash for thousands of years.
hp: Squash bee — co-evolved with Cucurbita for ~5,000 years (López-Uribe et al. 2016, PRSB). Dawn specialist. Nests directly below squash vines.

desc: Cherry mining bee — large, conspicuous spring mining bee with dense golden thoracic hair. Among the first bees flying each year, visiting cherry, plum, and other Rosaceae blooms. Ground-nesting in bare soil. Important early-season pollinator when few other bees are active. Scopal hairs on hind legs collect pollen.

fm.Size: ½ in–⅔ in; large for Andrena
fm.Hair: Dark; dense golden thorax; pale hind leg scopae
fm.Nesting: Ground; solitary; often in paths and banks

hp: Pollinator of fruit trees. Ground-nesting.

desc: Creosote resin bee — specialist bee of Larrea tridentata (creosote bush). Collects plant resins to line nest cells — gives cells a distinctive aromatic quality. Ground-nesting in desert soils. One of several bees tightly co-evolved with this dominant Mojave Desert shrub.

fm.Nesting: Ground; lines cells with Larrea resin (diagnostic behavior)

hp: Specialist on creosote bush (larrea tridentata). On Larrea (creosote). Nest associate.

desc: California carpenter bee — medium-large carpenter bee with metallic blue-green sheen on dark body. Smaller than the Valley Carpenter Bee (X. varipuncta). Excavates nests in dead agave stalks, yucca stems, and soft dead wood. Desert and desert-edge habitats. Buzz pollinates.

fm.Hair: Dark with blue-green metallic sheen; minimal pale hair
fm.Nesting: Excavates wood; also nests in yucca/agave stalks

hp: Agave; pollinator; yucca. Desert habitat. On Yucca (yucca). Nest associate.

desc: Pomona sweat bee — tiny brilliant metallic green bee. One of the few eusocial (colony-forming) sweat bees. Ground-nesting. Multiple females share a nest. Important wildflower pollinator.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Preys on ant. Metallic coloring.

desc: Perdita minima — among the smallest bees on Earth at under 2mm. Specialist on spurge (Euphorbia) flowers. Nearly invisible without magnification. Ground-nesting in sandy soil.

fm.Color: Dark; nearly smallest bee in world

hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting.

desc: Cactus bee — specialist buzz pollinator of Opuntia (prickly pear) cactus. Nests in dense ground aggregations near cactus stands. Male bees sleep in cactus flowers, which close at night. Desert and desert-edge habitats. Critical for Opuntia fruit set.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Opuntia (prickly pear) cactus flowers. On Opuntia (cactus). Nests in dense ground aggregations near cactus stands.

desc: Haynes's mining bee — medium mining bee common and conspicuous on Encelia (bush sunflower) and other Asteraceae in coastal sage scrub. Spring-flying. Ground-nesting in aggregations. Important pollinator of coastal sage scrub wildflowers. Named for early CA collector.

fm.Hair: Dark; pale thoracic pile; pale scopae

hp: Sunflower; sage; pollinator. Associated with Black Sage and Bush Sunflower.

desc: Red-footed digger bee — robust bee that uniquely collects floral oils from Krameria (ratany) flowers using specialized leg hairs. Rapid buzzy flight. Desert margins.

fm.Pollen: Scopae; also collects oils from Krameria

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Desert habitat. Robust.

desc: Mealy sweat bee — larger than most halictids, with distinctive pale pruinose (powdery/mealy) thoracic hair giving a flour-dusted appearance. Common on Asteraceae. Semi-social — foundress queens start nests alone, workers assist later in season. Named 'farinosus' (floury).
hp: Stored product pest of flour and dried foods. On Asteraceae (composites).

desc: Urban digger bee — fast-flying bee nesting in vertical clay banks and road cuts, extending nest entrance with distinctive mud chimney turret. Buzz pollinates tomatoes and other solanaceous crops. Common in urban gardens and parks. Important native pollinator throughout SoCal.

fm.Nesting: Clay banks; builds chimney extension at entrance; often in dense aggregations on road cuts

hp: Buzz pollinator — vibrates flowers for pollen. On tomato. Nest associate.

desc: Sunflower chimney bee — large, fast-flying bee with dense pale thoracic hair. Specialist of sunflowers and Asteraceae. Builds chimney-like turrets over ground nest entrances. Males patrol sunflower patches aggressively. Important native pollinator of Helianthus crops.

fm.Hair: Dense pale cream thorax; banded abdomen

hp: Specialist pollinator of Helianthus (sunflower) and Asteraceae. Ground-nesting. On Asteraceae (composites).

desc: Blueberry mason bee — brilliant metallic blue mason bee specializing on Ribes (currant/gooseberry) and blueberry flowers. Cavity nester in beetle borings and hollow stems. Commercially managed for blueberry pollination in some regions. More efficient per-flower than honeybees.

fm.Nesting: Cavity nester; bee hotels; pre-existing holes

hp: Blueberry mason bee — most efficient per-bee pollinator of blueberry (Sampson et al. 2004). Solitary cavity nester. Also on Ribes.

desc: Alfalfa leafcutter bee — small gray bee, the world's most important managed solitary bee pollinator. Introduced from Eurasia. Cuts circular leaf pieces. Single female pollinates more alfalfa per visit than dozens of honeybees. Nests in provided tubes on commercial farms.
hp: On alfalfa (Medicago sativa) — agricultural associate. Nest associate.

desc: Bellflower resin bee — uses plant resins and wood pulp instead of leaf pieces to build nest cells, distinguishing it from typical leafcutters. Cavity nester in beetle borings, hollow stems, and masonry crevices. Visits bellflower (Campanula) and many composites.

fm.Nesting: Cavities — uses plant resins not leaves

hp: Pollinator of Asteraceae (composites) — sunflowers, daisies, sagebrush.

desc: Tepanec long-horned bee — males with spectacularly long antennae (nearly body length). Fast, buzzy flight between sunflowers. Ground-nesting. Important native pollinator of Helianthus and other large composites. Females collect pollen on dense hind-leg scopae.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Helianthus (sunflower) and Asteraceae. Ground-nesting. On Asteraceae (composites).

desc: Pure green sweat bee — brilliant metallic green bee, unusual among halictids for nesting in rotting wood rather than soil. Solitary (unusual — most halictids are semi-social). Excavates cells in punky logs and stumps. Small but visually striking. Eastern species at range edge in SoCal.

fm.Nesting: Rotting wood — unusual for a sweat bee

hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Wood nester. Preys on ant. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Flat digger bee — robust, densely furred bee nesting in sandy ground banks. Important spring pollinator of manzanita, ceanothus, and other chaparral wildflowers. Fast, strong flight with loud buzz. Tongue long enough for deep flowers. Males patrol nest sites.
hp: Ceanothus; pollinator; manzanita. Associated with Bigberry Manzanita and Bigpod Ceanothus.

desc: Japanese mason bee introduced for orchard pollination. Metallic blue-green. Extremely efficient fruit pollinator.
hp: Japanese hornfaced bee — introduced from Japan for orchard pollination. Cavity nester. More cold-tolerant than native Osmia species.

desc: Western leafcutter bee — black bee with conspicuous white hair bands on abdomen. Cuts neat circular leaf pieces with mandibles in seconds. Ventral scopa bright orange-white. Important native pollinator of alfalfa — more efficient per visit than honeybees.
hp: On alfalfa (Medicago sativa) — agricultural associate. Family Megachilidae.

desc: Southern globe mallow bee — ground-nesting specialist building chimney-like turrets over nest entrance in bare soil. Closely associated with Sphaeralcea (globe mallow). Dense aggregation nester — hundreds of chimneys in suitable habitat. Desert and desert-edge.
hp: Globe mallow chimney bee — builds chimney-turret nest entrance in bare soil. Specialist on Sphaeralcea (globe mallow). Desert-edge nester.

desc: Sisymbrium sweat bee — one of the most common native bees in LA County, visiting an enormous range of wildflowers. Tiny metallic green. Ground-nesting in bare soil. Named for Sisymbrium (hedge mustard). Semi-social. Despite anonymity, critically important by sheer pollinator abundance.

fm.Nesting: Ground — dense aggregations in compacted soil
fm.Season: Feb–Nov; nearly year-round in mild areas

hp: Native wildflower and crop pollinator. Ground-nesting.

desc: Intrepid sweat bee — small dark sweat bee with semi-social nesting — multiple females share a common nest entrance but provision individual cells. Common garden visitor attracted to perspiration (hence 'sweat bee'). Generalist pollinator of native and ornamental flowers.
hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Generalist pollinator of native and ornamental flowers.

desc: Dialictus sweat bee — tiny dark metallic sweat bee. Enormous subgenus — hundreds of species across NA, many undescribed and requiring microscopic examination of female head sculpture for ID. Despite anonymity, collectively among the most abundant and important native pollinators.
hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Preys on ant. Metallic coloring.

desc: Punctate sweat bee — small dark metallic bee with finely punctate metasoma (abdomen). One of the most common native bees in LA County. Generalist visiting buckwheat, sunflowers, and many other plants. Ground-nesting. Semi-social. Despite tiny size, an important pollinator by sheer abundance.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Helianthus (sunflower) and Asteraceae. Ground-nesting. On Eriogonum (buckwheat).

desc: Black-eyed Susan mining bee — specialist bee closely associated with Rudbeckia and related Asteraceae. Nests in large aggregations in sandy soil — hundreds of nest entrances in bare patches. Critical pollinator for native coneflowers. Males patrol flower patches.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with pale hair bands

hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. On Asteraceae (composites).

desc: Green-tinged mining bee — small mining bee with slight green metallic tinge. Spring flier on willows and early wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Important early pollinator.
hp: Early spring pollinator on Salix (willow) catkins. Ground-nesting. Metallic coloring.

desc: Giant resin bee — large (22mm) Asian resin bee now established in California. Nests aggressively in wood cavities, displacing native Osmia and Megachile. Uses tree sap and wood pulp for nest partitions. Mandibles powerful enough to chew through soft wood. Conservation concern for native cavity-nesters.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Cavity nester. Preys on ant.

desc: California blue mason bee — metallic blue-green, the state's most important native orchard pollinator after honeybees. Cavity nester in beetle borings, hollow stems, and mason bee houses. More efficient per-flower than honeybees on apple, cherry, and almond blossoms.

fm.Nesting: Cavities — existing holes in wood, stems

hp: Pollinator of fruit trees. Cavity nester.

desc: Taurus mason bee — metallic blue mason bee accidentally introduced from Japan, now spreading across NA. Nests in existing holes in wood and masonry. Competes with native Osmia for nest cavities. Male with horn-like tubercles on face (hence 'taurus'). Generalist pollinator.
hp: Generalist pollinator of wildflowers. Wood nester.

desc: Western honey bee — the world's most important managed pollinator, introduced from Europe in the 1600s. Eusocial — perennial colonies of 20,000-60,000 workers. Feral colonies common throughout LA in tree cavities, walls, and utility boxes. Africanized genetics present in SoCal since 1990s.

fm.Nesting: Cavities — feral colonies in trees, buildings

hp: Managed pollinator — $15B+ annual US crop pollination value (Calderone 2012, Envir Entomol). Feral colonies common. Africanized genetics in SoCal.

desc: Eight-toothed cuckoo bee — cleptoparasitic bee with sharply pointed abdomen used to slice into leafcutter bee nest cell walls. Lays egg through the slit. Cuckoo larva kills host egg with large mandibles and eats stored pollen. Named for the eight abdominal teeth.

fm.Color: Black with white hair bands; pointed abdomen

hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Cleptoparasitic bee with sharply pointed abdomen used to sli.

desc: Bicolored striped sweat bee — female has brilliant metallic green head and thorax with yellow-and-black banded abdomen. One of the most recognizable and photogenic native bees. Ground-nesting. Common garden visitor. Semi-social — shared nest entrances. Widespread across NA.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. In gardens.

desc: Mesilla masked bee — tiny black bee with diagnostic yellow face markings. Unique among bees: carries pollen internally in crop (not on body hairs). Nests in hollow stems and beetle borings. Produces cellophane-like cell lining. One of the most abundant native bees in LA gardens.

fm.Color: Black with yellow face marks; wasp-like

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Preys on ant. Nest associate. Nests in hollow stems and beetle borings.

desc: Bracted plasterer bee — medium brown bee with pale hair bands, lining nest cells with a waterproof cellophane-like polyester secretion (unique to family Colletidae). Ground-nesting. On Asteraceae. The cellophane lining protects brood from soil fungi and waterlogging.

fm.Description: Medium brown furry bee that lines nest cells with cellophane-like secretion

hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. On Asteraceae (composites).

desc: California digger bee — robust, densely furred bee nesting in vertical clay banks and adobe walls. Extremely fast flight with deep buzz audible from several feet. Important native pollinator. Males establish territories near nest aggregations. Tongue reaches deep flowers.

fm.Description: Robust furry bee nesting in clay banks

hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Preys on ant. Nest associate. Important native pollinator.

desc: Arizona carpenter bee — large metallic blue-black carpenter bee (desert subspecies). Nests in dead agave flower stalks, yucca stems, and soft dead wood. Buzz pollinates — vibrates flowers to release pollen from poricidal anthers. Less destructive to structures than X. varipuncta.
hp: Agave; pollinator; yucca. Desert habitat. On Yucca (yucca). Nest associate.

desc: Texas nocturnal bee — remarkable sweat bee active at dusk and dawn, with enlarged ocelli (simple eyes) for low-light vision. Visits evening primrose (Oenothera) flowers that open at twilight. One of very few truly crepuscular bees. Ground-nesting. Extremely uncommon in LA.

fm.Color: Dark with enlarged ocelli for low light

hp: Specialist on Oenothera (evening primrose). Crepuscular pollinator. On Rosa (rose). Nest associate.

desc: Cactus wood-boring bee — small dark bee that bores into dead wood for nesting but visits cactus flowers exclusively for pollen. An unusual combination of nesting and foraging specializations. On Opuntia and Cylindropuntia. Desert and desert-edge habitats.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Opuntia (prickly pear) cactus flowers. On Opuntia (cactus). Nest associate.

desc: Orange-legged sweat bee — medium golden-brown halictid. Exhibits remarkable geographic variation in sociality: eusocial with queens and workers in warm areas like LA, solitary at high elevations and latitudes. A model organism for studying the evolution of social behavior in bees.

fm.Nesting: Ground — social in warm areas, solitary in cool

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Solitary.

desc: Parkinson's small carpenter bee — small dark carpenter bee nesting in flower stalks, agave stems, and pithy branches. Less destructive than large Xylocopa. Buzz pollinates. Named for the plant genus Parkinsonia (palo verde). Desert and desert-edge throughout SoCal.

fm.Description: Small dark carpenter bee nesting in flower stalks and soft pithy stems

hp: Agave; pollinator. Desert habitat. Associated with Desert Agave.

desc: Spotted nomad bee — red-and-yellow wasp-mimicking cleptoparasitic bee. Enters mining bee (Andrena) nests while the forager is absent, lays eggs on provisions. Nomad larva has sickle-shaped mandibles to kill host egg. Lacks pollen-collecting structures entirely.
hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Bee mimic. Family Apidae.

desc: Davis's blood bee — striking cleptoparasite with red abdomen and black head/thorax. Enters Lasioglossum (sweat bee) nests, kills host egg, lays own. Larva consumes provisions intended for host. Named for blood-red coloring. Wasp-like appearance — lacks pollen-collecting structures.
hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Family Halictidae.

desc: Brilliant plasterer bee — ground-nesting colletid lining nest cells with waterproof cellophane-like secretion. Specialist on buckwheat (Eriogonum) flowers. Aggregation nester in sandy soil. The clear cell lining is a biopolyester chemically similar to commercial plastic wrap.
hp: Pollinator of Eriogonum (buckwheat). Important native plant-pollinator link.

desc: Active long-horned bee — medium bee with extremely long male antennae (characteristic of Eucerini tribe). Fast buzzy flight between flowers. Ground-nesting. Important generalist pollinator of wildflowers. Distinguished from Melissodes by larger size and different facial structure.

fm.Color: Brown-gray; males with very long antennae

hp: Generalist pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Preys on ant.

desc: Spine-tipped small carpenter bee — tiny metallic blue-green bee nesting in pithy plant stems (elderberry, sumac). Mothers exhibit extended parental care — guarding larvae and provisioning progressively rather than mass-provisioning like most solitary bees. Among the smallest carpenter bees.
hp: In conifer forests (pinus). Associated with Coulter Pine and Blue Elderberry.

desc: Pale mining bee — medium-sized, densely pale-furred Andrena active in early spring. Important generalist pollinator of native wildflowers when few other bees are flying. Ground-nesting in bare soil. Scopal hairs on hind legs collect pollen. Common in chaparral and sage scrub.
hp: Pollinator; sage. Chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. Preys on ant.

desc: Western cuckoo bee — boldly black-and-white patterned cleptoparasitic bee. Parasitizes long-horned bees (Melissodes, Svastra). Female enters host nest while forager is away, lays egg. Cuckoo larva kills host egg and eats provisions. Wasp-like — lacks pollen-collecting apparatus.
hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Family Apidae. Boldly black-and-white patterned cleptoparasitic bee. Parasi.

desc: Hairy-banded mining bee — medium mining bee with conspicuous bands of dense white scopal hairs on legs. Spring-flying on willows and native shrubs. Ground-nesting in bare soil, often in aggregations. Early-season pollinator critical for willow catkin reproduction.

fm.Color: Black with dense pale scopal hairs

hp: Early spring pollinator on Salix (willow) catkins. Ground-nesting. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Honey-bellied striped sweat bee — female entirely brilliant metallic green; male has metallic green head and thorax but yellow-and-black banded abdomen. Ground-nesting in bare soil. Semi-social — shared nest entrances. One of the most recognizable and beautiful LA bees.

fm.Color: ♀ all green; ♂ green head + yellow abdomen

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Preys on ant.

desc: Robust pale desert bee. Males patrol nesting aggregations competing for emerging females. Palo verde specialist.
hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats. Nest associate.

desc: Pacific digger bee — robust black and yellow-haired bee closely resembling a bumblebee. Fast erratic flight with audible buzz. Nests in clay cliffs, road cuts, and adobe walls, sometimes in dense aggregations of hundreds. Important buzz pollinator of native wildflowers.

fm.Color: Black with yellow thoracic hair — bumblebee mimic

hp: Buzz pollinator — vibrates flowers for pollen. Preys on ant.

desc: Metallic green sweat bee — bright metallic green halictid with pale leg hairs forming scopae. Ground-nesting in bare soil. Generalist — visits wide range of flower types. Semi-social colonies with division of labor. One of the most visually striking small bees in LA gardens.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. In gardens. Metallic.

desc: Pollen wasp — not a bee but convergently evolved to collect pollen. A masarid wasp that feeds larvae pollen-and-nectar paste (other wasps feed larvae insects). Specialist on Phacelia flowers. Wasp body plan but bee-like ecology. Mud-pot nester. Desert and chaparral.

fm.Behavior: Pollen wasp — convergent evolution with bees

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Chaparral habitat. Desert habitat. Preys on insects.

desc: Medium furry mining bee with prominent facial hair. Ground-nesting in sandy soil. Spring wildflower pollinator.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with pale thoracic hair

hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Ground-nesting in sandy soil.

desc: Nason's mining bee — small mining bee famously nesting in sidewalk cracks and compacted urban soil. One of the earliest bees flying each spring, visiting dandelions and early wildflowers. Dense urban nesting aggregations. Important early-season urban pollinator.
hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Preys on ant. Nest associate. Dense urban nesting aggregations.

desc: Mallow specialist mining bee — oligolectic bee specializing on Malvaceae flowers, particularly globe mallow (Sphaeralcea) and checkerbloom (Sidalcea). Scopal hairs adapted for mallow's large spiny pollen grains. Ground-nesting. Spring-flying. Coastal sage scrub and chaparral.
hp: Sage; grain. Chaparral. Associated with Black Sage and Desert Globemallow.

desc: Dark blue mason bee — deep metallic blue-black mason bee. Nests in beetle borings, hollow stems, and woodpecker holes. Lines cells with masticated leaf pulp. Important early-spring pollinator of fruit trees, willflowers, and garden plants. Single annual brood.
hp: Pollinator of fruit trees. Cavity nester. Prey for woodpeckers.

desc: Mountain mason bee — metallic blue mason bee of montane meadows in San Gabriel Mountains. Nests in abandoned beetle borings in dead wood. Lines cells with masticated leaf pulp. Important wildflower pollinator above 5,000 ft. Single spring-summer brood.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Wood nester. Preys on ant. Montane.

desc: Giant resin bee — large, powerfully built leafcutter bee with impressively strong mandibles. Uses plant resins mixed with stone chips and sand to construct nest cell partitions — a unique building material among megachilids. Cavity nester. On native wildflowers.

fm.Nesting: Cavities — uses resin and stone chips

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. In buildings. Nest associate.

desc: Rock digger bee — robust bee nesting directly in sandstone and soft rock cliff faces. Excavates individual cells in stone substrate — an unusual nesting strategy among bees. Desert and canyon habitats. Fast powerful flight. On desert wildflowers including Larrea and Penstemon.
hp: Specialist on Larrea tridentata (creosote bush). Desert pollinator.

desc: Western furrow bee — medium sweat bee with distinctive orange-tinted legs. Semi-social — shared nests with cooperative brood care. Common garden visitor on many flower types. Named 'hesperus' (western). Distinguished from H. ligatus by leg coloring and western range.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. In gardens. Nest associate.

desc: Titus's sweat bee — small metallic halictid. Guard bees stationed at nest entrance, blocking entry with their heads — a form of social behavior. Sociality varies with latitude: more social in warmer climates. Important pollinator by collective abundance despite tiny individual size.
hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Preys on ant. Guard bees stationed at nest entrance, blocking entry.

desc: Sumptuous sweat bee — vivid metallic gold-green bee. One of the more strikingly colored native bees. Ground-nesting. Visits a wide variety of flowers in gardens and chaparral.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. In gardens. Chaparral habitat.

desc: California fairy bee — among the smallest bees in the world at 3-4mm. Bright yellow and black. Ground-nesting in sandy soil. Various species in the enormous genus Perdita are extreme flower specialists. Desert and coastal scrub. Easily mistaken for a tiny wasp.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Desert habitat. Coastal habitats.

desc: Agile long-horned bee — fast-flying bee with extremely long male antennae. Specialist of Asteraceae, especially sunflowers. Ground-nesting. Dense hind-leg scopae transport large pollen loads. Important late-summer pollinator when many spring bees have finished flying.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Helianthus (sunflower) and Asteraceae. Ground-nesting. On Asteraceae (composites). Long-horned.

desc: Modest masked bee — tiny (5mm) black bee with white face markings, often mistaken for a small wasp. Like all Hylaeus, carries pollen internally in crop rather than on body hairs. Nests in hollow stems and insect borings. Despite tiny size, an important pollinator by abundance.

fm.Color: Black with white face marks; wasp-like

hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Cavity nester. Preys on ant. Tiny.

desc: Two-spotted long-horned bee — dark bee with two pale felt-like spots on abdominal tergites. Males with very long antennae (body length). Important late-season pollinator of sunflowers, coneflowers, and other composites when spring bees have finished. Ground-nesting.

fm.Color: Dark with two pale abdominal spots; ♂ long antennae

hp: Specialist pollinator of Helianthus (sunflower) and Asteraceae. Ground-nesting. On Asteraceae (composites). Family Apidae.

desc: Medium fuzzy mining bee with greenish-tinted abdomen. Important pollinator of spring wildflowers and fruit trees.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with pale thoracic hair

hp: Green-bellied mining bee — early spring pollinator of fruit trees and native wildflowers. Ground-nesting in bare soil. Important orchard pollinator.

desc: Opuntia cactus bee — ground-nesting specialist of prickly pear flowers, building distinctive mud chimney turrets over nest entrances. Dense aggregation nester. Critical for cactus fruit production. Males compete for females at cactus flowers. Desert and desert-edge throughout SoCal.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Opuntia (prickly pear) cactus flowers. On Opuntia (cactus). Nest associate. Dense aggregation nester.

desc: Willow mining bee — among the earliest bees flying each spring, specializing on willow catkins that bloom before most other flowers. Critical early-season pollinator providing the first pollen of the year for nest provisioning. Ground-nesting in aggregations near willow stands.
hp: Early spring pollinator on Salix (willow) catkins. Ground-nesting. Critical early-season pollinator providing the first pollen of.

desc: Pale-bellied woolcarder — native wool carder bee that scrapes plant trichomes (leaf hairs) with mandibles to build nest material. Less aggressive than the introduced European A. manicatum. Cavity nester. On native wildflowers in chaparral. Yellow and black.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Chaparral habitat.

desc: Alkali bee — medium bee with stunning iridescent green-blue abdominal bands. The most important managed native bee pollinator — commercial alfalfa production depends on dense nesting aggregations in prepared alkaline soil beds. Ground-nesting. Magnificent under magnification.

fm.Nesting: Ground — huge aggregations in alkaline soil

hp: Important alfalfa pollinator. Ground-nesting.

desc: Incomplete sweat bee — small dark metallic halictid. Extremely common throughout LA County but easily overlooked due to tiny size (5mm). Generalist flower visitor. Semi-social — multiple females may share a nest entrance with individual brood cells. Important by collective abundance.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers and garden plants. Preys on ant.

desc: Dark-legged oil bee — robust digger bee that collects floral oils from Krameria (ratany) in addition to pollen. Oil mixed with pollen for larval provisions — unusual nutrition strategy. Ground-nesting. Desert-edge habitats. At northern range limit in LA County.
hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats. Nest associate.

desc: Black mining bee with distinctive gray thoracic hair. Solitary ground-nester. Spring specialist — among the first bees active each year. Visits willows, fruit trees.
hp: Early spring pollinator on Salix (willow) catkins. Ground-nesting. Family Andrenidae. Solitary ground-nester.

desc: Unusual bee that collects floral oils instead of nectar. Specialist on loosestrife (Lysimachia) flowers. Waterproofs nest cells with oil.

fm.Behavior: Collects floral oils — waterproofs brood cells

hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers and garden plants. Nest associate.

desc: Large bee specializing on squash/pumpkin flowers. Active at dawn — often found sleeping inside closed squash blossoms by midday. Critical crop pollinator.

fm.Habitat: Squash and pumpkin flowers — dawn specialist
fm.Behavior: Sleeps inside closed squash blossoms by midday

hp: Specialist pollinator of Cucurbita (squash, pumpkin). Dawn-active.

desc: Small wasp-like bee (virtually hairless, no pollen baskets). Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bees' nests; larva kills host egg and eats provisions. Social parasite.

fm.Color: Red-orange and black; wasp-like; no body hair
fm.Behavior: Cleptoparasite — enters other bee nests to lay eggs

hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Family Apidae. Lays eggs in other bees' nests; larva kills host egg and eat.

desc: Black-and-white felt-patterned cuckoo bee. Parasitizes Colletes bees. Thick white hair patches form distinctive pattern. No pollen-collecting apparatus.
hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Family Apidae.

desc: Desert calliopsis — tiny specialist bee of desert wildflowers. Ground-nesting in sandy soil. Part of diverse desert bee fauna.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Desert habitat. Nest associate.

desc: Tiny bee (3-5mm) specializing on evening primrose (Oenothera). One of the smallest solitary bees. Ground-nesting. Active primarily at dawn.
hp: Specialist on Oenothera (evening primrose). Crepuscular pollinator. On Rosa (rose). Nest associate (3-5mm).

desc: Large fuzzy golden bee resembling a teddy bear. Strong fast flier. Nests in soft sandstone and mud banks. Recently documented in SoCal.
hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Nest associate. Nests in soft sandstone and mud banks.

desc: Leafcutter bee with orange-red ventral scopa. Visits sunflowers and other composites. Common in late summer gardens.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Helianthus (sunflower) and Asteraceae. Ground-nesting. On Asteraceae (composites). Family Megachilidae.

desc: Metallic green bee. ♀ green head+thorax with striped abdomen. ♂ entirely green. Generalist pollinator. Ground-nesting.
hp: Generalist pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Metallic.

desc: Black mining bee with dense black hair. Spring specialist on willow catkins. One of the first bees flying each year.

fm.Color: Black with dense black body hair

hp: Early spring pollinator on Salix (willow) catkins. Ground-nesting. Family Andrenidae. Mining.

desc: Illinois mining bee — medium mining bee with distinctive white facial hair (clypeal brush). Spring generalist visiting many wildflower species. Ground-nesting in bare soil patches, lawns, and path margins. Important early pollinator. Named for Illinois (type locality).
hp: Generalist pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Preys on ant. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Not actually a bee — parasitoid wasp that attacks solitary bee nests. Included here because it's most commonly seen at bee nesting sites. Thread-waisted.
hp: Parasitoid of solitary bee/wasp nests. Only.

desc: Medium bee secreting cellophane-like lining for nest cells. Specialist on composites (sunflower family). Fall-flying.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Helianthus (sunflower) and Asteraceae. Ground-nesting. On Asteraceae (composites). Family Colletidae.

desc: Tiny yellow-and-black bee specializing on desert flowers. Ground-nesting in sand. One of the smallest bees in the world.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Desert habitat. Smallest.

desc: Medium bee — ♂ with extremely long antennae that wrap around during sleep. Nests in ground. Specialist on legume flowers.
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. Family Apidae.

desc: Horse-fly carpenter bee — metallic blue-black carpenter bee. Smaller than Valley carpenter bee. Nests in dead wood and plant stems. Fast powerful flier. Important pollinator.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Wood nester. Preys on ant. Metallic coloring.

desc: Fine striped sweat bee — brilliant metallic green head/thorax, ♀ with striped abdomen. Very common at flowers. Ground-nesting. Important urban pollinator.

fm.Color: ♀ green head/thorax + striped abdomen; ♂ all green

hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Preys on ant. Urban.

desc: Arizona small carpenter bee — tiny metallic blue-green bee nesting in pithy stems. Breaks open dead stems to create nest cells. Semi-social — mother guards larvae.
hp: Native wildflower and crop pollinator. Stem nester.

desc: Bindweed turret bee — medium fuzzy bee building tall chimney turrets over soil nest entrance. Specialist pollinator of morning glory and bindweed (Convolvulus). Common in weedy urban lots.

fm.Habitat: Bindweed/morning glory — chimney turrets on soil

hp: On morning glory (Ipomoea/Convolvulus) — Convolvulaceae.

desc: California digger-cuckoo bee — large black-and-white bee. Cleptoparasite of Anthophora digger bees. Enters host nests to lay eggs. Hovers near nesting banks scouting hosts.

fm.Behavior: Cleptoparasite — scouts digger bee nesting banks

hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Large black-and-white bee. Cleptoparasite of Anthophora digg.

desc: Sumac mining bee — tiny yellow-and-black specialist bee on sumac (Rhus) and lemonade berry. One of the many Perdita species endemic to California. Ground-nesting in sandy soil.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting.

desc: Squash bee — large robust bee specializing on squash/pumpkin flowers. Active at dawn — enters squash flowers early. Males sleep inside closed blossoms. Ground-nesting.
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. On Fraxinus (ash). Robust.

desc: Sweet nomad bee — small wasp-like cuckoo bee. Cleptoparasite of Andrena mining bees — enters host nests to lay eggs. Red-orange and black; virtually hairless.
hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Family Apidae. Small wasp-like cuckoo bee. Cleptoparasite of Andrena mining.

desc: Short digger bee — robust fast-flying bee nesting in clay banks. Important native pollinator. Hovers like a hummingbird. Active in spring on salvia and other flowers.
hp: Pollinator of Salvia (sage) and Lamiaceae. Native plant-pollinator association.

desc: Faithful leafcutter bee — medium leafcutter bee cutting circular pieces from leaves to line nest cells. Carries pollen on ventral scopa. Common in late summer gardens.

fm.Color: Dark; pale abdominal bands; orange belly scopa

hp: Native wildflower and crop pollinator. Leafcutter — lines cells with leaf pieces. Nest associate.

desc: Oval-headed sweat bee — tiny dark sweat bee with oval head shape. Ground-nesting. Generalist pollinator. Very common but easily overlooked.
hp: Generalist pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting.

desc: Colorado mason bee — small metallic blue-green mason bee. Nests in pre-existing holes. Important spring pollinator of native wildflowers in foothill habitats.
hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Preys on ant. Nest associate. Nests in pre-existing holes.

desc: California poppy fairy bee — tiny specialist bee on California poppies. Among the smallest bees — under 5mm. Ground-nesting in sandy soil near poppy fields. Spring flight.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. On California poppies.

desc: Northern rotund-resin bee — small round bee using plant resin to construct nests. Black with yellow markings. Nests in pre-existing cavities. Visits composite flowers.

fm.Color: Black with yellow spots; round body

hp: Native pollinator. Uses resin for nest construction. Preys on ant. Family Megachilidae.

desc: Spotted woolcarder — medium bee with bold yellow spots on dark body. Males aggressively defend flower territories by body-slamming intruders. Females card plant fibers for nests.
hp: Native pollinator. Cards plant fibers for nest. Preys on ant. Family Megachilidae.

desc: Globe mallow bee — medium fuzzy bee specializing on globe mallow (Sphaeralcea). Orange mallow pollen loads diagnostic. Desert-urban interface.

fm.Color: Fuzzy gray-brown; orange mallow pollen loads

hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats. Urban.

desc: Bumblebee-like digger bee — large robust bee that closely resembles a bumblebee. Nests in clay banks. Fast powerful flier. Visits many flowers. Important native pollinator.

fm.Color: Fuzzy black and yellow — bumblebee mimic

hp: Associated with Bombus (bumblebees) — parasite or predator.

desc: Saddest digger bee — large robust bee nesting in soil banks. Despite the name (tristissima = 'saddest'), it's an active vigorous pollinator of spring wildflowers.
hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Nest associate. Despite the name (tristissima = 'saddest'), it's an.

desc: Verbesina longhorn-cuckoo bee — cleptoparasitic bee. Lays eggs in nests of longhorn bees (Melissodes). Spiny abdomen. Distinctively patterned black-and-white.

fm.Color: Black with white hair patches; spiny abdomen

hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Spiny/armed.

desc: Rose-loving calliopsis — small mining bee visiting evening primrose and other flowers. Ground-nesting. Males patrol flower patches. Desert-urban interface.
hp: Specialist on Oenothera (evening primrose). Crepuscular pollinator. On Rosa (rose). Nest associate. Rose-loving.

desc: Illustrated woolcarder bee — large colorful bee with bold yellow markings. Collects plant fibers ('wool') for nests. Males territorial on flower patches.
hp: Native pollinator. Cards plant fibers for nest. Preys on ant.

desc: Collected woolcarder — medium woolcarder bee with yellow spots. On various flowers. Nests with plant fiber. Part of the diverse California Anthidium fauna.
hp: Native pollinator. Cards plant fibers for nest. Preys on ant. Family Megachilidae.

desc: Blue-phacelia miner — mining bee visiting phacelia and other wildflowers. Spring specialist. Ground-nesting in bare soil. Important native wildflower pollinator.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Preys on ant. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Punctate masked bee — tiny wasp-like bee with yellow face marks and punctured integument. Nests in beetle holes and hollow stems. Carries pollen internally.

fm.Color: Black with yellow face; virtually hairless

hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Cavity nester.

desc: Red-tailed micro shortface — tiny halictid bee. Among the smallest bees in LA County. Ground-nesting. Specialist on phacelia flowers. Red-tipped abdomen.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Ambushes prey on flowers. Smallest.

desc: Bearded calliopsis — small mining bee with distinctive facial hair. Ground-nesting in sandy soil. Visits evening primrose and other wildflowers.
hp: Specialist on Oenothera (evening primrose). Crepuscular pollinator. On Rosa (rose). Nest associate. Ground-nesting in sandy soil.

desc: Clarkia evening bee — small bee specializing on Clarkia flowers. Part of the small family Melittidae. Ground-nesting. Active in late afternoon. California native specialist.
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. Family Melittidae.

desc: Baker's phacelia shortface — tiny halictid specializing on phacelia. Among the smallest bees. Ground-nesting in sandy soil. Named for entomologist C.F. Baker.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Smallest.

desc: Grinnell's small mason bee — small mason bee nesting in pre-existing holes in wood and stems. Named for zoologist Joseph Grinnell. On native wildflowers.
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Cavity/stem nester. Family Megachilidae.

desc: Frugal leafcutter bee — medium leafcutter cutting precise circles from leaves. Carries pollen on ventral scopa. Visits various flowers. Common in gardens.

fm.Color: Dark; pale bands; orange ventral scopa

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. In gardens. Family Megachilidae.

desc: Yellow-collared masked bee — tiny wasp-like bee with yellow collar and face marks. Nests in hollow stems. Carries pollen internally in crop. Visits many flowers.

fm.Color: Black with yellow collar and face marks

hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Cavity/stem nester. Tiny.

desc: California chelostoma — small dark mason bee nesting in beetle holes. Specialist on Phacelia flowers. Ground-nesting in pre-existing cavities. California native.
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. Family Megachilidae.

desc: Lonely mining bee — solitary ground-nesting bee of spring wildflower habitats. Visits various flowers. Part of the enormous Andrena genus (~80 species in California).
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. Solitary. Enormous.

desc: Common longhorn bee — medium bee with long ♂ antennae. Visits sunflower family flowers in late summer. Ground-nesting. Sleeps on flower heads.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Helianthus (sunflower) and Asteraceae. Ground-nesting.

desc: Slevin's cellophane bee — solitary bee secreting cellophane-like polyester lining for nest cells. Ground-nesting. Waterproof and antimicrobial nest coating. On native flowers.
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. Solitary. Family Colletidae.

desc: Parallel leafcutter bee — large leafcutter with orange ventral scopa. Cuts circular pieces from leaves. One of the largest North American leafcutters.
hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Largest.

desc: Shining small-mason bee — small metallic green-blue mason bee. Nests in beetle holes and hollow stems. Important native pollinator of wildflowers.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Cavity nester. Preys on ant. Metallic coloring.

desc: Pomona longhorn bee — medium bee named for Pomona, CA. Squash specialist. Males sleep inside closed squash flowers. Ground-nesting. Dawn flier.
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. On Fraxinus (ash). Family Apidae.

desc: White-fronted small-mason — tiny mason bee with white face. Nests in pre-existing holes in wood or stems. Spring wildflower pollinator.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Wood nester.

desc: Oblique longhorn bee — medium bee with long ♂ antennae on late-summer composites. Important pollinator of native sunflowers.
hp: Specialist pollinator of Helianthus (sunflower) and Asteraceae. Ground-nesting. On Asteraceae (composites). Family Apidae.

desc: Los Angeles leafcutter bee — leafcutter bee named for Los Angeles. Cuts circular leaf pieces to line nest cells. On native flowers. California species.
hp: Native pollinator. Cuts leaf pieces for nest cells.

desc: Red-flower slender mason — tiny mason bee on red-flowering plants. Nests in beetle holes. Important native pollinator of foothill wildflowers.
hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Preys on ant. Nest associate. Nests in beetle holes.

desc: Unusual-bodied mining bee — small ground-nesting bee with distinctive body shape. On native wildflowers. Part of California's diverse native bee fauna.
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. Family Andrenidae.

desc: Nimble squash bee — medium bee specializing on squash and pumpkin flowers. Active at dawn before other bees. Ground-nesting near squash plantings.
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. On Fraxinus (ash). Family Apidae.

desc: Golden-haired mining bee — medium bee with golden thoracic hair. Spring-flying. Ground-nesting. Important pollinator of early wildflowers.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Preys on ant. Family Andrenidae.

desc: Black-legged mining bee — medium dark bee with diagnostic jet-black legs (most Andrena have pale leg hairs). Ground-nesting in bare soil. Spring-flying on native wildflowers and Rosaceae. Important early-season pollinator in coastal sage scrub and chaparral openings.
hp: Pollinator; sage. Chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. On Rosa (rose).

desc: Edwards' digger bee — large robust fast-flying bee. Nests in clay banks. Important native pollinator visiting many flower types. Hovers like a hummingbird.
hp: Native pollinator — visits diverse wildflowers for pollen and nectar. Visited by/prey for hummingbirds.

desc: Crotch's digger bee — medium robust bee nesting in soil. Named for pioneer West Coast entomologist G.R. Crotch. On various flowers. Spring flier.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers and garden plants. Nest associate.

desc: Desdemona fairy bee — extremely tiny specialist bee. Among the smallest bees in the world (~3mm). Ground-nesting in sandy soil. On specific wildflowers.

fm.Color: Yellow and black; one of world's smallest bees

hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting.

desc: Apical leafcutter — medium leafcutter bee cutting circular pieces from rose and other leaves. Introduced from Europe. Nests in pre-existing holes.
hp: On Rosa (roses) — garden and wild species. On Rosa (rose). Nest associate.

desc: Three-signed fairy bee — tiny (~4mm) specialist bee on lotus and related plants. Ground-nesting. Among the world's smallest bees.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Lotus.

desc: Fiddleneck longhorn bee — medium bee with long ♂ antennae specializing on fiddleneck (Amsinckia) flowers. Spring flier.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers and garden plants. Family Apidae.

desc: California resin-leafcutter — medium bee combining resin and leaf pieces for nest construction. Unusual double-material nesting. California native.

fm.Color: Dark; uses both resin AND leaf pieces for nest

hp: Native wildflower and crop pollinator. Resin nester.

desc: Spotted cuckoo bee — small cleptoparasitic bee with spotted black-and-white body. Targets ground-nesting bees (Andrena, Lasioglossum). Female enters host nest and lays egg. Cuckoo larva destroys host egg and consumes stored pollen provisions. Uncommon.
hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Family Apidae. Small cleptoparasitic bee with spotted black-and-white body.

desc: Crescent longhorn bee — medium bee with ♂ long antennae. On various wildflowers. Spring-flying. Ground-nesting.
hp: Native wildflower pollinator. Ground-nesting. Family Apidae.

desc: Formula nomad bee — small red-and-black cleptoparasitic bee convincingly mimicking a wasp. Enters Andrena mining bee nests during the host's foraging trips. Lacks pollen-collecting apparatus — entirely dependent on host provisions. Multiple species parasitize different Andrena hosts.
hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Bee mimic. Family Apidae.

desc: Creosote digger-cuckoo bee — cleptoparasitic bee targeting Anthophora nests near creosote bush. Black with white hair patches.
hp: Creosote; parasitoid of bees. Associated with Creosote Bush.

desc: Crotch's nomad bee — small wasp-like cuckoo bee. Red-and-yellow. Parasitizes mining bee (Andrena) nests. Named for entomologist G.R. Crotch.
hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Family Apidae. Small wasp-like cuckoo bee. Red-and-yellow. Parasitizes mini.

desc: Creosote small-mason — tiny mason bee associated with creosote bush habitat. Nests in beetle holes. Desert specialist.
hp: Specialist on creosote bush (larrea tridentata). On Larrea (creosote). Nest associate.

desc: Davidson's longhorn bee — medium bee with very long ♂ antennae. Named for early CA collector Davidson. On native flowers.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers and garden plants. Family Apidae.

desc: Ehrhorn's rotund-resin bee — tiny round bee using plant resin for nest construction. On native flowers. Named for entomologist Ehrhorn.

fm.Color: Black with yellow spots; round body

hp: Native pollinator. Uses resin for nest construction. Preys on ant. Tiny.

desc: Singular cuckoo bee — cleptoparasitic bee entering host nests (Diadasia turret bees) to lay eggs. Uncommon. On desert flowers scouting host nests.
hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests.

desc: Mesilla cuckoo bee — small cleptoparasitic bee targeting Colletes cellophane bee nests. Red-and-black wasp-like appearance.
hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Family Apidae. Small cleptoparasitic bee targeting Colletes cellophane bee.

desc: Woodland mason bee — small dark mason bee of woodland habitats. Nests in dead wood. Important native pollinator.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Wood nester. Preys on ant. Woodland.

desc: Wide-striped dark bee — small cleptoparasitic bee targeting Megachile and Osmia nests. Dark body with pale bands. Enters host cavities and lays egg among stored provisions. Stelis larvae have enlarged mandibles for destroying host eggs. Part of the 'cuckoo megachilid' guild.

fm.Color: Dark with wide pale bands; parasitic

hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Small cleptoparasitic bee targeting Megachile and Osmia nest.

desc: Separated cuckoo bee — large black-and-white cleptoparasitic bee targeting Anthophora digger bee nests. Hovers near nesting banks.

fm.Color: Black with white hair patches; parasitic

hp: Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bee nests. Large black-and-white cleptoparasitic bee targeting Anthopho.

desc: Conspicuous masked bee — tiny black bee with yellow face markings ('mask'). Nests in beetle holes. Carries pollen internally in crop. Unusual for bees.
hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Nest associate.

desc: Robust longhorn bee — medium bee with long ♂ antennae. On composites in late summer. Ground-nesting. Important late-season pollinator.
hp: Pollinator of Asteraceae (composites) — sunflowers, daisies, sagebrush. Ground-nesting.

desc: Olive mining bee — medium bee with subtle olive-green tinge to thoracic hair. Ground-nesting in bare soil. Generalist spring pollinator of native wildflowers. Part of the enormous genus Andrena — over 1,500 species worldwide, making it the largest bee genus.
hp: Generalist pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. On olive.

desc: Splendid mason bee — small metallic blue-green mason bee with iridescent sheen. Nests in beetle borings and hollow stems. Named 'aglaia' for one of the Greek Graces. On native wildflowers in chaparral and foothill habitats. Single spring brood. Lines cells with chewed leaf pulp.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Chaparral habitat. Metallic. Nest associate.

desc: Yellow-banded digger bee — robust fuzzy bee with yellow thoracic bands. Fast flier. Ground-nesting. Important native pollinator.
hp: Native pollinator of wildflowers. Ground-nesting. Preys on ant. Densely hairy.
